skill.name: share
skill.description: Create or update shareable, commentable p11 documents. Use when Codex should author a polished proposal, brief, report, spec, plan, memo, or review draft and publish it with p11.
skill.summary: Create shareable, commentable p11 documents from plain text using a React-based document format.
skill.author: rarexlabs

security_audit.summary: All 51 static findings are false positives. The 4 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' alerts target YAML description strings and a React component name (DefinitionDescription) containing the substring 'DES'. The 47 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' alerts are markdown code formatting backticks, not shell operators. The skill does legitimately invoke CLI commands (p11, npm) as part of its document publishing workflow, which is the basis for the external_commands risk factor. No malicious intent, data exfiltration, or prompt injection detected.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm - False Positive (React Component Name)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'DefinitionDescription' in references/components.md:34 as a weak cryptographic algorithm. This is a React component name from @p11-core/components containing the substring 'DES' (Data Encryption Standard). It is not an actual cryptographic operation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm - False Positive (YAML Description Strings)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged description strings in agents/openai.yaml:3, SKILL.md:3, and SKILL.md:5 as weak cryptographic algorithms. These are plain-text YAML description fields with no cryptographic references.

low_findings.title: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution - False Positive (Markdown Code Blocks)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged backtick characters across SKILL.md and references/components.md as Ruby/shell backtick execution. All instances are markdown code fence delimiters (```) or inline code markers (`) used for formatting code examples and documentation. No shell execution backticks exist in these files.

content.user_title: Create and Share Polished p11 Documents
content.value_statement: Creating shareable, reviewable documents with comments usually requires multiple tools and complex workflows. This skill lets you author polished documents as React code and publish them with a shareable link using the p11 CLI.
actual_capabilities: Create new p11 documents from text prompts as shareable .tsx files
Update existing p11 documents and publish new versions using edit URLs
Generate proposals, briefs, reports, specs, plans, memos, and review drafts
Validate documents for correct component usage before publishing
Provide both shareable read URLs and private edit URLs for published documents
Format documents with React components including headings, sections, tables, and code blocks
limitations: Requires the p11 CLI to be installed in the execution environment
Documents must use only document-safe components from @p11-core/components
Cannot include interactive elements such as forms, buttons, or navigation
Does not support local preview of documents before publishing

use_cases.title: Draft and Share a Project Proposal
use_cases.description: Turn meeting notes into a formatted proposal document with sections, headings, and tables, then publish it for team review.

use_cases.title: Publish Technical Documentation
use_cases.description: Create structured documentation with code examples and share it with a commentable link for developer feedback.

use_cases.title: Create Reviewable Specification Documents
use_cases.description: Author detailed spec documents that stakeholders can review inline with comments before approval.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Document
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a p11 document with the title 'Weekly Status Report' containing sections for accomplishments, blockers, and next steps.
prompt_templates.scenario: New user creating their first document

prompt_templates.title: Document with Tables and Lists
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a p11 document comparing three solutions. Include a feature comparison table and a bullet list of pros and cons for each option.

prompt_templates.title: Update an Existing Document
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the existing report at report.tsx using the edit URL provided. Add a new section for Q4 results with a data table.
prompt_templates.scenario: Updating published documents with new content

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Format Document with Code Examples
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a technical specification document with a title page, an introduction section, a code example using CodeBlock, and a requirements table. Use pageless mode for continuous scrolling.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced document formatting with mixed content types

output_examples.output: I will create a p11 document for your project kickoff. After running p11 share, you will receive a read URL for viewing and commenting, and an edit URL for future updates.

output_examples.input: Update the Q3 report with new revenue numbers
output_examples.output: I will update the existing document at the provided edit URL. A new version will be published preserving comments on unchanged sections while adding the revenue data table.

output_examples.input: Create a meeting notes document with action items
output_examples.output: I will create a structured meeting notes document with sections for attendees, discussion points, decisions made, and an action items table with assignees and deadlines.

best_practices: Always verify the p11 CLI is installed and up to date before creating or updating documents.
Use only document-safe components from @p11-core/components to ensure documents render correctly.
Keep edit URLs private and share only read URLs for document review.
anti_patterns: Do not include interactive React components like forms, buttons, or navigation in p11 documents.
Do not install the p11 CLI as a project dependency; use global installation or npx instead.
Do not expose edit URLs in logs or to reviewers; they grant update and delete access.

faq.question: What is a p11 document?
faq.answer: A p11 document is a static React .tsx module that uses document-safe components for creating shareable, commentable content.

faq.question: Do I need to install anything?
faq.answer: Yes, the p11 CLI must be installed globally. The skill can install it for you with npm if it is missing.

faq.question: Can I edit a document after publishing?
faq.answer: Yes, use the edit URL from the original publish to push updated versions. The read URL stays the same.

faq.question: What components can I use in a p11 document?
faq.answer: Use document-safe components from @p11-core/components including Document, Heading, Section, Text, Table, CodeBlock, and more.

faq.question: Are my documents private?
faq.answer: Published documents are accessible to anyone with the read URL. Edit URLs act as bearer credentials and should be kept private.

faq.question: Can I delete a published document?
faq.answer: Yes, you can delete a document using its edit URL. Keep the edit URL safe if you may need to delete the document later.
